quote: I was just giving you a, call because we had a time set up to share a solar design with you, but that had fallen through. So we were just seeing if there would be a better day, next week that would work for you.
stewart_read: Ty frames the entire call as a scheduling errand. On a reschedule call — where the customer already no-showed or was missed once — the value proposition needs to be re-lit, not assumed. Ty had a natural opening to briefly re-anchor why the appointment is worth Michael's time before asking for a new slot. He did not take it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Ty may have judged that re-pitching on a reschedule call risks re-opening objections and that a clean, low-friction rebook is the better play. That logic has merit on a warm lead — but the absence of any value re-anchor still leaves the appointment structurally fragile.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Ty what he would say if Michael had asked 'remind me why I agreed to this.' If Ty can't answer quickly, the reschedule script needs a one-sentence value re-anchor before the time ask.

quote: Oh, tomorrow is Friday. Yeah. We should be able to do tomorrow for sure. I thought it was Saturday for some reason. My fault.
stewart_read: Ty self-corrects a day-of-week error on a call that is already a reschedule of a prior missed appointment. Two scheduling reliability signals in under two minutes — the customer now has implicit evidence that the rep's calendar management is loose. Ty's 'My fault' is honest but does nothing to rebuild confidence.
coaching_implication: Flag for the handoff brief. The closer should open with a confident, specific confirmation of the time to reset the reliability signal. In the 1-on-1, coach Ty to verify the date before dialing on reschedule calls — a 10-second check eliminates this entirely.

quote: It should be Matthew that's meeting with you. It might be someone different. It'll get it might get changed tomorrow, so we'll see.
stewart_read: Ty introduces two layers of uncertainty in the same breath — who the closer will be, and whether that will change. 'So we'll see' is the most trust-eroding phrase in the call. On a reschedule, the customer's implicit question is 'will this actually happen this time?' Ty's answer, unintentionally, is 'maybe not exactly as described.'
alternative_hypothesis: Ty may be being transparent to avoid a worse surprise if Matthew is unavailable — honesty over false certainty. That instinct is reasonable, but the delivery compounds rather than manages the uncertainty.
coaching_implication: Coach Ty to name the closer with confidence and handle uncertainty privately. If the closer assignment is genuinely fluid, the script should be: 'You'll hear from our specialist — I'll make sure they have everything they need.' Naming uncertainty to the customer on a reschedule call raises no-show risk.

quote: Looks like he's pretty much open anytime after 1PM, so you can do anytime between one and six.
stewart_read: Ty never proactively asked whether anyone else is on the title or needs to be present for the appointment. The third-person reference here ('he's pretty much open') may reflect Ty checking an internal calendar, but it surfaces the absence of any co-decision-maker check across the entire call. On a reschedule touch, this is the natural moment to confirm: 'Is it still just you, or should we make sure your [spouse/partner] is on as well?' That question was never asked.
alternative_hypothesis: The prior appointment record may already have co-decision-maker status on file, making a re-ask redundant. Without visibility into what was captured on the original booking, this cannot be confirmed.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, establish whether Ty reviewed the original lead record before dialing. If co-decision-maker status was not confirmed on the original call either, this is a qualification gap that travels into the closer's appointment. Coach Ty to treat reschedule calls as a lightweight re-verify opportunity, not just a time swap.

quote: We will shoot your reminder text just to double check that time still works for you. We're not popping that out of the blue.
stewart_read: Ty promises a reminder text, which is a required button-up element. However, the text promise is the only button-up element completed. The call closes without the one-hour block statement, the computer/not-driving anchor, or the 'is that fair' verbal handshake — all of which are required per the codex and directly predictive of show rate.
coaching_implication: The text promise is a positive habit. Use it as the entry point in the 1-on-1: 'You remembered the text — good. Walk me through the rest of the button-up checklist.' The gap is not awareness of the text; it's that the text is the only anchor Ty placed on a structurally fragile reschedule appointment.

quote: But plan for him for now. But, yeah, I'll get that updated. I appreciate your time today, sir, and look forward to speaking with you tomorrow at one.
stewart_read: Ty closes the call without completing the required button-up protocol: no one-hour block stated, no computer/not-driving instruction, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake. On a first booking these are important; on a reschedule of a prior missed appointment, they are the primary tool available to reduce no-show risk. The close is warm in tone but structurally incomplete.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty may have judged that a short, frictionless close is better than a full protocol recitation on a customer who has already been through the process once. That read has some merit — but the 'is that fair' handshake in particular costs nothing and materially improves commitment.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, role-play the last 30 seconds of this call with the full button-up sequence inserted. The goal is for Ty to feel how little time it adds and how much firmer the close feels with the verbal handshake in place.
